figures.caption: Figure 1. On the left, the profile of a 2D isotropic P tensor field on a mesh with ne ‚Čą 5 √ó 106 triangular elements and N ‚Čą 107 indices. Next to it the corresponding profiles of some isotropic T fields with ¬Ķ = 9 (middle) and ¬Ķ = 16 (right) disjoint regions. The relative discrepancies ‚ą•P‚ąíT‚ą•2F/‚ą•P‚ą•2F are 50% and 35% respectively.

Figure 2. Statistics for some key error quantities as obtained by running algorithm 1 on 100 independent isotropic parameter choices on the two-dimensional problem. Columns 1 to 5 are on a logarithmic scale and correspond to ‚ą•Y‚ąí1 ‚ąí YŐĄ‚ąí1‚ą•/‚ą•Y‚ąí1‚ą•, ‚ą•Y ‚ąí YŐĄ‚ą•F/‚ą•Y‚ą•F, ‚ą•Y ‚ąí YB‚ą•F/‚ą•Y‚ą•F, ‚ą•wŐĄőł ‚ąí w‚ą•/‚ą•w‚ą•, and ‚ą•wŐĄ ‚ąí w‚ą•/‚ą•w‚ą•. The data in column 6 with linear scale refer to cV (P). The top panels show the variation of these errors at moderately small coefficients of variation at small (őĹ = 10) and larger (őĹ = 100) ensemble sizes, whilst those below refer to larger coefficients of variation, with (őĹ = 50) and (őĹ = 500). The panels on the left are with ¬Ķ = 1 and those on the right with ¬Ķ = 16. The relative error of our low-variance estimator wŐĄőł is always smaller than that of the multi-sketch average estimator wŐĄ, and this advantage is more pronounced in the small coefficient of variation regime.

Figure 3. Images of the spatial profile of an isotropic parameter field from the first set of tests with cV (P) = 0.40, ¬Ķ = 1 and őĹ = 100 (left), its deterministic projected FEM solution w (middle) and the absolute pointwise error |w ‚ąí wŐĄőł| from its sketched solution (right). Notice that the solution is spatially smooth and the absolute error in the sketched solution is predominantly very small apart from a few isolated areas.

Figure 4. Statistics for some key error quantities as obtained by running algorithm 1 on 100 independent anisotropic parameter choices on the three-dimensional problem. In each panel the columns 1 to 5 on a logarithmic scale are ‚ą•Y‚ąí1 ‚ąí YŐĄ‚ąí1‚ą•/‚ą•Y‚ąí1‚ą•, ‚ą•Y‚ąí YŐĄ‚ą•F/‚ą•Y‚ą•F, ‚ą•Y‚ąíYB‚ą•F/‚ą•Y‚ą•F, ‚ą•wŐĄőł ‚ąíw‚ą•/‚ą•w‚ą•, and ‚ą•wŐĄ‚ąíw‚ą•/‚ą•w‚ą•. The data on column 6 having a linear scale refer to the coefficient of variation cV (P). The results follow in the trend of those of the two-dimensional problem in fig. 2, in that in both cases the low-variance estimator (column 4) is on average more efficient than the multisketch average (column 5).
